perturbation.original_text: 19.3. Invoices are payable within 60 days of receipt, with the exception of payments due under Clause 19.2, which shall be payable on the due date or on acceptance of the work, which ever is the later.
perturbation.changed_text: 19.3. Invoices are payable within 30 days of receipt, with the exception of payments due under Clause 19.2, which shall be payable on the due date or on acceptance of the work, which ever is the later.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an in-text contradiction by modifying the standard payment terms. Originally, invoices were payable within 60 days, but the modification reduces this to 30 days. This directly contradicts Clause 14.2, which implicitly operates under a 60-day timeframe.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 14.2. In relation to sales the Publishers make direct, the Publishers shall establish copyright and other fees for such sales ("Article Fees"). HealthGate shall collect the Article Fees established by Publishers plus a service fee to be determined by HealthGate but in any event the service fee may not exceed 30% of the Article Fee for the particular article, or $US 4, whichever is the higher. Within 60 days of the end of each calendar month, HealthGate shall forward to Publishers the net Article Fees actually collected (exclusive of HealthGate's service fee).
perturbation.justification: The original text stated invoices are payable within 60 days. The changed text modifies this to 30 days. Clause 14.2 states that HealthGate shall forward net Article Fees within 60 days of the end of each calendar month. The change from 60 to 30 days directly contradicts the existing 60-day timeframe in clause 14.2, creating a clear in-text inconsistency regarding payment terms as per the 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: 17.1 The Publishers shall have the right to renew the term of the Services by notice in writing to HealthGate to be given on or before 30 September 1999. If the Publishers exercise their right to renew, the term of the Services shall be extended by one further year, up to and including 28 February 2001. The Use Fees shall remain the same as in the Initial Period and the fee for the Services shall not exceed $7000 for additional journals, $2000 maintenance fee on existing journals and $2000 per Gigabyte.
perturbation.changed_text: 17.1 The Publishers shall have the right to renew the term of the Services by notice in writing to HealthGate to be given on or before 30 September 1999. If the Publishers exercise their right to renew, the term of the Services shall be extended by one further year, up to and including 28 February 2001. The Use Fees shall be 10% higher than in the Initial Period and the fee for the Services shall not exceed $7000 for additional journals, $2000 maintenance fee on existing journals and $2000 per Gigabyte.
perturbation.explanation: This change creates an in-text contradiction concerning the Use Fees upon renewal. Originally, the Use Fees remained the same, but the modification states they will be 10% higher. This conflicts with the general understanding established in Clause 19.1, which implies a fixed price subject to the terms and conditions of the Agreement, thus creating uncertainty and potentially leading to disputes regarding the payable Use Fees during the renewal period.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 19.1. The total price payable by the Publishers is set out in Clause 19.2 and the Use Fees in Clause 19.4, subject to the terms and conditions in this Agreement, this price being a fixed price.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that 'Use Fees shall remain the same as in the Initial Period'. The changed text modifies this to state 'The Use Fees shall be 10% higher than in the Initial Period'. Clause 19.1 states 'the Use Fees in Clause 19.4, subject to the terms and conditions in this Agreement, this price being a fixed price.' Therefore, by changing the Use Fees, the modified text directly contradicts the concept of a fixed price established in Clause 19.1, creating a strong contradiction and potential unenforceability issue.

perturbation.original_text: 10.1. Proprietary Software HealthGate hereby grants to the Publishers a non-exclusive non-transferable licence to use the Proprietary Software for the purposes of this Agreement Save in relation to the Publishers' logos, trademarks, and content, HealthGate may use and/or licence the Proprietary Software for itself or for others without any compensation or liability to the Publishers. All Proprietary Software and Source Code remain the property of HealthGate. Publishers may not use either Proprietary Software or Source Code held in escrow to develop a product that competes with those services offered by HealthGate. HealthGate, in its sole discretion, retains the right to determine if Publishers are utilizing either the Proprietary Software or Source Code in violation of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 10.1. Proprietary Software HealthGate hereby grants to the Publishers an exclusive non-transferable licence to use the Proprietary Software for the purposes of this Agreement Save in relation to the Publishers' logos, trademarks, and content, HealthGate may not use and/or licence the Proprietary Software for itself or for others without any compensation or liability to the Publishers. All Proprietary Software and Source Code remain the property of HealthGate. Publishers may not use either Proprietary Software or Source Code held in escrow to develop a product that competes with those services offered by HealthGate. HealthGate, in its sole discretion, retains the right to determine if Publishers are utilizing either the Proprietary Software or Source Code in violation of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an in-text contradiction within Clause 10.1 itself and with Clause 10.2, regarding the exclusivity of the Proprietary Software license. It now states that HealthGate cannot use or license the software for others, which contradicts the license granted to the Publishers. This also makes clauses 10.2 irrelevant where the options for licence are discussed and where the clause refers to the licence in Clause 10.2.1, which there is no licence to refer to, since clause 10.1 grants exclusive rights.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 10.2. Option for Licence 10.2.1. On termination of the provision of the Services by HealthGate to the Publishers for whatever reason, HealthGate shall at the Publishers' option: (i) grant to the Publishers a non-exclusive non-transferable licence to use the Proprietary Software for the purposes of using, developing, enhancing and maintaining the Site and carrying out any or all of the activities previously carried out by HealthGate or on its behalf under this Agreement (ii) exercise best endeavours to grant to the Publishers a non-exclusive non-transferable licence to use the Third Party Software for the Site when and to the extent requested by the Publishers.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'non-exclusive' to 'exclusive' in Clause 10.1 directly contradicts Clause 10.2. While the original text allowed HealthGate to license the software to others while granting a non-exclusive license to the publishers, the changed text prohibits HealthGate from doing so. This makes clause 10.2, which outlines options for the Publishers to obtain a *non-exclusive* license upon termination, irrelevant because HealthGate is no longer able to grant such a license under 10.1. This creates a strong inconsistency regarding the availability and terms of the software license, causing a contradiction.
